question: explain logger.go and what calls it

notes.fact: Logger struct holds Out (io.Writer), Hooks (LevelHooks), Formatter, ReportCaller, Level, mu (MutexWrap), entryPool (sync.Pool), ExitFunc, BufferPool.
MutexWrap wraps sync.Mutex with a Disable() flag so locking can be turned off via SetNoLock.
New() returns *Logger seeded with os.Stderr, TextFormatter{}, empty LevelHooks, InfoLevel, os.Exit, ReportCaller=false.
newEntry/releaseEntry pool reusable *Entry values via logger.entryPool to reduce allocations on every log call.

WithField/WithFields/WithError/WithContext/WithTime allocate an Entry from the pool, delegate to entry.With*, then release the entry — but the *Entry returned by entry.With* is a freshly allocated copy, so it is safe to release.

Logf is the *fmt-formatted* log core: gates on IsLevelEnabled, pulls a pooled entry, calls entry.Logf, releases. Tracef/Debugf/Infof/Warnf/Errorf/Fatalf/Panicf delegate to Logf with their respective Level constants.
Log and LogFn are the *non-format* siblings of Logf — same gate-then-pool pattern. LogFn lazy-evaluates fn() only after the level check.
Print/Printf/Println/PrintFn skip IsLevelEnabled — they always emit. They call entry.Print/Printf/Println directly.
Fatal* variants call Logf/Log/Logln/LogFn at FatalLevel and then logger.Exit(1) — so the process exits even if FatalLevel is below the configured Level.
Panic* variants log at PanicLevel; the panic is actually triggered inside entry.log (entry.go:319-321), not here.
Exit runs alt_exit handlers via runHandlers() then defers to logger.ExitFunc (defaulting to os.Exit). Test code can override ExitFunc to assert Fatal* behavior without killing the process.
level/SetLevel/GetLevel use sync/atomic on logger.Level so reads from IsLevelEnabled are race-free without holding logger.mu.
hooksForLevel takes a snapshot copy of the hook slice under logger.mu, so callers iterate without holding the lock — required because hook.Fire can re-enter logging.
IsLevelEnabled is the only level gate: returns logger.level() >= level. Higher Level value = more verbose (TraceLevel=6 > InfoLevel=4).
SetFormatter/SetOutput/SetReportCaller/ReplaceHooks/SetBufferPool acquire logger.mu before mutating their target fields.

var std = New(); StandardLogger() returns it. Every package-level logging function (Trace/Debug/Info/Warn/Error/Panic/Fatal and their f/ln/Fn variants) is a thin wrapper that calls the same method on std.
SetOutput/SetFormatter/SetLevel/SetReportCaller/AddHook/IsLevelEnabled/GetLevel and WithError/WithContext/WithField/WithFields/WithTime are all delegations to std.<same method>.
Compile-time interface checks assert that *Logger, *Entry, and *log.Logger all satisfy StdLogger — this is what guarantees logrus is drop-in compatible with stdlib log.
Level constants are declared HERE, not in level.go. PanicLevel=0 ... TraceLevel=6 (iota order). Lower numeric value = more severe.
StdLogger / FieldLogger / Ext1FieldLogger interfaces — Logger satisfies all three. Libraries should accept FieldLogger to remain implementation-agnostic.
Entry is what actually carries a log event. It holds back-pointer to Logger (entry.Logger), Data fields, Time, Level, Caller, Message, Buffer, Context.
NewEntry(logger) — what logger.newEntry() falls back to when the pool is empty.

entry.log is where the real work happens: dup, set Time/Level/Message, snapshot reportCaller and bufferPool under logger.mu, fire hooks, format, write. If level <= PanicLevel it panics with the entry.
getBufferPool prefers logger.BufferPool if set, otherwise the package-global bufferPool from buffer_pool.go.
entry.write does the formatter dance (snapshot under lock, format outside lock, re-lock for the actual Out.Write) — fixes #1440 and #1448 (deadlock on reentrant logging).
entry.Log is the one IsLevelEnabled gate that all of logger.Log / logger.Trace / logger.Debug / etc. eventually exercise via entry.log.
Hook interface: Levels() + Fire(*Entry); LevelHooks is map[Level][]Hook; logger.Hooks is this map. AddHook adds to every level the hook subscribes to.
Package-global handlers slice + runHandlers; logger.Exit calls runHandlers() before logger.ExitFunc(code). RegisterExitHandler/DeferExitHandler append/prepend handlers.
Formatter interface = single method Format(*Entry) ([]byte, error). Logger.Formatter holds one; entry.write picks it up under logger.mu.
Package-level bufferPool (a defaultPool wrapping sync.Pool of *bytes.Buffer) is the fallback when logger.BufferPool is nil. SetBufferPool replaces it globally.
Logger.Writer / WriterLevel return an *io.PipeWriter that pipes lines into NewEntry(logger).WriterLevel(level) — used to redirect stdlib log.SetOutput into logrus.
